1	#!/bin/sh
2	echo "RBJ: Beginning sentry setup script"
3	
4	set -e
5	test "${RBJ_VERBOSE:-0}" -ge 1 && set -x
6	
7	echo "RBJp0: Validate compose env-file quoting"
8	: "${RBJE_PROBE:?}"
9	z_probe_count=0
10	for z_word in ${RBJE_PROBE}; do
11	  z_probe_count=$((z_probe_count + 1))
12	done
13	test "${z_probe_count}" -eq 2                || { echo "FATAL: RBJE_PROBE token count ${z_probe_count}, expected 2 — value: '${RBJE_PROBE:-}'"; exit 1; }
14	test "$(echo ${RBJE_PROBE} | cut -d' ' -f1)" = "alpha" || { echo "FATAL: RBJE_PROBE first token not 'alpha' — value: '${RBJE_PROBE:-}'"; exit 1; }
15	test "$(echo ${RBJE_PROBE} | cut -d' ' -f2)" = "bravo" || { echo "FATAL: RBJE_PROBE second token not 'bravo' — value: '${RBJE_PROBE:-}'"; exit 1; }
16	echo "RBJp0: Compose env-file quoting validated — RBJE_PROBE = '${RBJE_PROBE}'"
17	
18	echo "RBJp1: Validate parameters"
19	: "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BASE_IP:?}"        && echo "RBJp0: RBRN_ENCLAVE_BASE_IP        = ${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BASE_IP}"
20	: "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_NETMASK:?}"        && echo "RBJp0: RBRN_ENCLAVE_NETMASK        = ${RBRN_ENCLAVE_NETMASK}"
21	: "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP:?}"      && echo "RBJp0: RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP      = ${RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP}"
22	: "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP:?}"      && echo "RBJp0: RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP      = ${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP}"
23	: "${RBRR_DNS_SERVER:?}"             && echo "RBJp0: RBRR_DNS_SERVER             = ${RBRR_DNS_SERVER}"
24	: "${RBRN_ENTRY_MODE:?}"             && echo "RBJp0: RBRN_ENTRY_MODE             = ${RBRN_ENTRY_MODE}"
25	: "${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION:?}" && echo "RBJp0: RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION = ${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}"
26	: "${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE:?}"     && echo "RBJp0: RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE     = ${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE}"
27	: "${RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE:?}"        && echo "RBJp0: RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE        = ${RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE}"
28	: "${RBRN_UPLINK_PORT_MIN:?}"        && echo "RBJp0: RBRN_UPLINK_PORT_MIN        = ${RBRN_UPLINK_PORT_MIN}"
29	: "${RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE:?}"     && echo "RBJp0: RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE     = ${RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE}"
30	: "${RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_CIDRS:?}"   && echo "RBJp0: RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_CIDRS   = ${RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_CIDRS}"
31	: "${RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_DOMAINS:?}" && echo "RBJp0: RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_DOMAINS = ${RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_DOMAINS}"
32	
33	echo "RBJp1: Discovering network interfaces by IP (Docker does not guarantee eth0/eth1 ordering)"
34	z_temp_file="/tmp/rbj_iface_discovery.txt"
35	
36	ip -o addr show to "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP}" > "${z_temp_file}" || exit 11
37	read z_num RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF z_rest < "${z_temp_file}"
38	test -n "${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF}" || { echo "FATAL: No interface found with IP ${RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP}"; exit 11; }
39	
40	ip -o -4 addr show scope global > "${z_temp_file}" || exit 11
41	RBJ_UPLINK_IF=""
42	while read z_num z_ifname z_rest; do
43	  test "${z_ifname}" = "${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF}" && continue
44	  RBJ_UPLINK_IF="${z_ifname}"
45	  break
46	done < "${z_temp_file}"
47	test -n "${RBJ_UPLINK_IF}" || { echo "FATAL: No uplink interface found"; exit 11; }
48	
49	rm -f "${z_temp_file}"
50	echo "RBJp1: Enclave interface = ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF}"
51	echo "RBJp1: Uplink interface  = ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF}"
52	
53	# Docker assigns the container's default route to whichever attached network's
54	# gateway it picks (alphabetically-first attached, empirically); compose 'priority'
55	# is not honored. Same don't-trust-Docker discipline as interface naming above —
56	# sentry takes ownership: derive uplink gateway as first usable IP of uplink
57	# subnet (Docker bridge convention) and install default route via uplink.
58	echo "RBJp1: Computing uplink gateway from connected subnet"
59	z_uplink_addr_cidr=$(ip -o -4 addr show dev "${RBJ_UPLINK_IF}" | awk '{print $4; exit}')
60	test -n "${z_uplink_addr_cidr}" || { echo "FATAL: No IPv4 address on uplink interface ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF}"; exit 12; }
61	z_uplink_ip="${z_uplink_addr_cidr%/*}"
62	z_uplink_prefix="${z_uplink_addr_cidr#*/}"
63	
64	IFS=. read z_o1 z_o2 z_o3 z_o4 <<EOF_OCTETS
65	${z_uplink_ip}
66	EOF_OCTETS
67	
68	z_host_bits=$(( 32 - z_uplink_prefix ))
69	z_ip_int=$(( (z_o1 << 24) | (z_o2 << 16) | (z_o3 << 8) | z_o4 ))
70	z_mask=$(( 0xFFFFFFFF ^ ((1 << z_host_bits) - 1) ))
71	z_net_int=$(( z_ip_int & z_mask ))
72	z_gw_int=$(( z_net_int + 1 ))
73	RBJ_UPLINK_GW="$(( (z_gw_int >> 24) & 0xFF )).$(( (z_gw_int >> 16) & 0xFF )).$(( (z_gw_int >> 8) & 0xFF )).$(( z_gw_int & 0xFF ))"
74	echo "RBJp1: Uplink gateway     = ${RBJ_UPLINK_GW}"
75	
76	echo "RBJp1: Replacing default route via uplink (sentry-side ownership)"
77	ip route replace default via "${RBJ_UPLINK_GW}" dev "${RBJ_UPLINK_IF}" || exit 13
78	ip -o -4 route show default
79	
80	echo "RBJp1: Beginning IPTables initialization"
81	
82	echo "RBJp1: Set ephemeral port range for uplink connections"
83	echo "${RBRN_UPLINK_PORT_MIN} 65535" > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_local_port_range || exit 10
84	
85	echo "RBJp1: Flushing existing rules"
86	iptables -F        || exit 10
87	iptables -t nat -F || exit 10
88	
89	echo "RBJp1: Setting default policies"
90	iptables -P INPUT   DROP || exit 10
91	iptables -P FORWARD DROP || exit 10
92	iptables -P OUTPUT  DROP || exit 10
93	
94	echo "RBJp1: Configuring loopback access"
95	iptables -A INPUT  -i lo -j ACCEPT || exit 10
96	iptables -A OUTPUT -o lo -j ACCEPT || exit 10
97	
98	echo "RBJp1: Setting up connection tracking"
99	iptables -A INPUT   -m state --state RELATED,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT || exit 10
100	iptables -A FORWARD -m state --state RELATED,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT || exit 10
101	iptables -A OUTPUT  -m state --state RELATED,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT || exit 10
102	
103	echo "RBJp1: Creating RBM chains"
104	iptables -N RBM-INGRESS || exit 10
105	iptables -N RBM-EGRESS  || exit 10
106	iptables -N RBM-FORWARD || exit 10
107	
108	echo "RBJp1: Setting up chain jumps"
109	iptables -A INPUT   -j RBM-INGRESS || exit 10
110	iptables -A OUTPUT  -j RBM-EGRESS  || exit 10
111	iptables -A FORWARD -j RBM-FORWARD || exit 10
112	
113	echo "RBJp2: Allowing ICMP within enclave only"
114	iptables -A RBM-INGRESS -i ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -p icmp -j ACCEPT || exit 20
115	iptables -A RBM-EGRESS  -o ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -p icmp -j ACCEPT || exit 20
116	
117	echo "RBJp2: Enabling IP forwarding (required whenever sentry forwards on behalf of the enclave)"
118	echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward || exit 25
119	
120	if test "${RBRN_ENTRY_MODE}" = "rbnne_enabled"; then
121	  echo "RBJp2c: Relaxing rp_filter to loose mode (required for interface-agnostic ingress)"
122	  # rp_filter=1 (strict) requires the packet's source-IP reverse-path to match the
123	  # ingress interface. Inbound published-port traffic from the framework gateway
124	  # (e.g., Docker Desktop 192.168.65.1) arrives on whichever interface Docker chose
125	  # for port-publish delivery, but its reverse route is always via the default
126	  # gateway on uplink — strict mode mismatches and drops at routing time, before
127	  # the iptables FORWARD chain ever sees the packet. Loose mode (rp_filter=2)
128	  # allows any source IP that's reachable via any interface, which is the actual
129	  # invariant for entry-port traffic. Iptables rules below still gate authorization;
130	  # rp_filter is not the load-bearing security control.
131	  echo 2 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/all/rp_filter || exit 25
132	
133	  echo "RBJp2c: Configuring entry-port DNAT + MASQUERADE + FORWARD-ACCEPT"
134	  # Classification: destination port + per-IP source exclusion of the two enclave-internal
135	  # containers (sentry and bottle/pentacle). RETURN short-circuit pattern — if source matches
136	  # either enclave container IP, skip the DNAT; otherwise the unconditional DNAT below fires.
137	  #
138	  # Why per-IP and not whole-CIDR (! -s ENCLAVE_CIDR): on linux Docker Engine the host
139	  # attaches to the enclave bridge as a peer with the bridge gateway IP (.1), which is inside
140	  # the enclave CIDR by Docker convention. Whole-CIDR exclusion rejects every legitimate host
141	  # SYN (empirically confirmed on cerebro: PREROUTING DNAT fired 0 times). Per-IP excludes
142	  # exactly the threat surface — the two enclave container IPs known to compose — and lets
143	  # the bridge gateway, Desktop's VM gateway (192.168.65.1), rootlesskit's synthetic source
144	  # (10.0.2.100), and external clients all pass.
145	  #
146	  # Invariant defended: "enclave-internal sources MUST NOT reach sentry's entry port via DNAT"
147	  # (ifrit's direct_sentry_probe and net_dnat_entry_reflection sorties).
148	  iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -p tcp --dport "${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}" \
149	           -s "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP}" -j RETURN || exit 25
150	  iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -p tcp --dport "${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}" \
151	           -s "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP}" -j RETURN || exit 25
152	  iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -p tcp --dport "${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}" \
153	           -j DNAT --to-destination "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP}:${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE}" || exit 25
154	
155	  # Return-path symmetry: MASQUERADE rewrites the source of the post-DNAT packet to
156	  # sentry's enclave IP, so the bottle's reply destination is in-enclave. The bottle then
157	  # routes via its directly-connected enclave route back to sentry, where conntrack reverses
158	  # the NAT cleanly. Without this, the bottle would reply to the original (possibly
159	  # framework-rewritten) source IP, traversing default-via-sentry into transit — empirically
160	  # broken on Docker Desktop per the topology-reframe scry diagnostic.
161	  iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -o ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -p tcp \
162	           -d "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP}" --dport "${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE}" -j MASQUERADE || exit 25
163	
164	  # Authorization: only flows that sentry's own PREROUTING DNAT created conntrack state
165	  # for are allowed to forward. Conntrack DNAT-state is unforgeable from either bridge.
166	  # The parent FORWARD chain's ESTABLISHED,RELATED ACCEPT (line above) handles the return
167	  # path before reaching RBM-FORWARD.
168	  iptables -A RBM-FORWARD -p tcp \
169	           -d "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP}" --dport "${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE}" \
170	           -m conntrack --ctstate DNAT -j ACCEPT || exit 25
171	fi
172	
173	echo "RBJp2b: Blocking ICMP cross-boundary traffic"
174	iptables -A RBM-FORWARD         -p icmp -j DROP || exit 28
175	iptables -A RBM-EGRESS  -o ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} -p icmp -j DROP || exit 28
176	
177	echo "RBJp3: Phase 3: Access Setup"
178	if test "${RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE}" = "rbnne_disabled"; then
179	  echo "RBJp3: Blocking all non-port traffic"
180	  iptables -A RBM-EGRESS  -o ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} -j DROP || exit 30
181	  iptables -A RBM-FORWARD -i ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -j DROP || exit 30
182	else
183	  echo "RBJp3: Setting up uplink network hardening"
184	  echo 0 > /proc/sys/net/ipv6/conf/all/disable_ipv6    || exit 31
185	  echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/${RBJ_UPLINK_IF}/route_localnet || exit 31
186	
187	  echo "RBJp3: Configuring NAT"
188	  iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -o ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} -s "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BASE_IP}/${RBRN_ENCLAVE_NETMASK}" \
189	                                       ! -d "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BASE_IP}/${RBRN_ENCLAVE_NETMASK}" \
190	                                       -j MASQUERADE || exit 31
191	
192	  if test "${RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE}" = "rbnne_global"; then
193	    echo "RBJp3: Enabling global access"
194	    iptables -A RBM-EGRESS  -o ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} -j ACCEPT || exit 31
195	    iptables -A RBM-FORWARD -i ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -j ACCEPT || exit 31
196	  else
197	    echo "RBJp3: Configuring DNS server access"
198	    iptables -A RBM-EGRESS  -o ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} -p udp --dport 53 -d "${RBRR_DNS_SERVER}"        -j ACCEPT || exit 31
199	    iptables -A RBM-EGRESS  -o ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} -p tcp --dport 53 -d "${RBRR_DNS_SERVER}"        -j ACCEPT || exit 31
200	    iptables -A RBM-FORWARD -i ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -p udp --dport 53 -d "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP}" -j ACCEPT || exit 31
201	    iptables -A RBM-FORWARD -i ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -p tcp --dport 53 -d "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP}" -j ACCEPT || exit 31
202	    iptables -A RBM-FORWARD -i ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -p udp --dport 53                                -j DROP   || exit 31
203	    iptables -A RBM-FORWARD -i ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -p tcp --dport 53                                -j DROP   || exit 31
204	
205	    echo "RBJp3: Setting up CIDR-based access control"
206	    for cidr in ${RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_CIDRS}; do
207	      iptables -A RBM-EGRESS  -o ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} -d "${cidr}" -j ACCEPT || exit 32
208	      iptables -A RBM-FORWARD -i ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -d "${cidr}" -j ACCEPT || exit 32
209	    done
210	  fi
211	fi
212	
213	echo "RBJp4: Configuring DNS services"
214	
215	echo "RBJp4: Configuring sentry DNS resolution"
216	echo "nameserver ${RBRR_DNS_SERVER}" > /etc/resolv.conf   || exit 40
217	
218	if test "${RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE}" = "rbnne_disabled"; then
219	  echo "RBJp4: Blocking all DNS traffic"
220	  iptables -A RBM-FORWARD -i ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -p udp --dport 53 -j DROP || exit 40
221	  iptables -A RBM-FORWARD -i ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -p tcp --dport 53 -j DROP || exit 40
222	  iptables -A RBM-EGRESS  -o ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} -p udp --dport 53 -j DROP || exit 40
223	  iptables -A RBM-EGRESS  -o ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} -p tcp --dport 53 -j DROP || exit 40
224	else
225	  echo "RBJp4: Set up DNS Server"
226	
227	  echo "RBJp4: Note version in use"
228	  dnsmasq --version
229	
230	  echo "RBJp4: Configuring dnsmasq"
231	  echo "bind-interfaces"                                         > /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
232	  echo "interface=${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF}"                             >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
233	  echo "listen-address=${RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP}"               >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
234	  echo "no-dhcp-interface=${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF}"                     >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
235	  echo "dns-forward-max=150"                                    >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
236	  echo "cache-size=1000"                                        >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
237	  echo "min-port=4096"                                          >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
238	  echo "max-port=65535"                                         >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
239	  echo "min-cache-ttl=600"                                      >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
240	  echo "max-cache-ttl=3600"                                     >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
241	  echo "no-resolv"                                              >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
242	  echo "strict-order"                                           >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
243	  echo "bogus-priv"                                             >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
244	  echo "domain-needed"                                          >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
245	  echo "except-interface=${RBJ_UPLINK_IF}"                       >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
246	  echo "log-queries=extra"                                      >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
247	  echo "log-facility=/var/log/dnsmasq.log"                      >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
248	  echo "log-dhcp"                                               >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
249	  echo "log-debug"                                              >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
250	  echo "log-async=20"                                           >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
251	  if test "${RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE}" = "rbnne_global"; then
252	    echo "RBJp4: Enabling global DNS resolution"
253	    echo "server=${RBRR_DNS_SERVER}"                          >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
254	  else
255	    echo "RBJp4: Resolve-then-freeze — resolve allowed domains via upstream, freeze as static entries"
256	    for domain in ${RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_DOMAINS}; do
257	      echo "RBJp4: Resolving ${domain} via ${RBRR_DNS_SERVER}"
258	      z_ips=$(dig +short A "${domain}" @"${RBRR_DNS_SERVER}" 2>/dev/null | grep -E '^[0-9]+\.' | sort -u)
259	      test -n "${z_ips}" || { echo "FATAL: Cannot resolve ${domain} via ${RBRR_DNS_SERVER}"; exit 42; }
260	      for z_ip in ${z_ips}; do
261	        echo "RBJp4: Freezing ${domain} -> ${z_ip}"
262	        echo "address=/${domain}/${z_ip}"                    >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
263	      done
264	    done
265	    # No server= lines — no forwarding path exists. Subdomain queries return frozen IPs.
266	    # All non-allowed domains get NXDOMAIN.
267	    echo "address=/#/"                                        >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
268	  fi
269	
270	  echo "RBJp4: Echo back the constructed dnsmasq config file"
271	  cat                                                              /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
272	
273	  echo "RBJp4: Starting dnsmasq service"
274	  dnsmasq
275	  echo "RBJp4: Waiting for dnsmasq to initialize"
276	  sleep 2
277	
278	  echo "RBJp4: Configuring DNS firewall rules"
279	  iptables -A RBM-INGRESS -i ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -p udp --dport 53                         -j ACCEPT || exit 43
280	  iptables -A RBM-INGRESS -i ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -p tcp --dport 53                         -j ACCEPT || exit 43
281	  iptables -A RBM-EGRESS  -o ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} -p udp --dport 53 -d "${RBRR_DNS_SERVER}" -j ACCEPT || exit 43
282	  iptables -A RBM-EGRESS  -o ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} -p tcp --dport 53 -d "${RBRR_DNS_SERVER}" -j ACCEPT || exit 43
283	fi
284	
285	echo "RBJp5: Signaling health"
286	touch /tmp/rbjh_healthy || exit 50
287	
288	echo "RBJp5: Sentry setup complete, entering hold"
289	exec sleep infinity
290	
291

file.content:
#!/bin/sh
echo "RBJ: Beginning sentry setup script"

set -e
test "${RBJ_VERBOSE:-0}" -ge 1 && set -x

echo "RBJp0: Validate compose env-file quoting"
: "${RBJE_PROBE:?}"
z_probe_count=0
for z_word in ${RBJE_PROBE}; do
  z_probe_count=$((z_probe_count + 1))
done
test "${z_probe_count}" -eq 2                || { echo "FATAL: RBJE_PROBE token count ${z_probe_count}, expected 2 — value: '${RBJE_PROBE:-}'"; exit 1; }
test "$(echo ${RBJE_PROBE} | cut -d' ' -f1)" = "alpha" || { echo "FATAL: RBJE_PROBE first token not 'alpha' — value: '${RBJE_PROBE:-}'"; exit 1; }
test "$(echo ${RBJE_PROBE} | cut -d' ' -f2)" = "bravo" || { echo "FATAL: RBJE_PROBE second token not 'bravo' — value: '${RBJE_PROBE:-}'"; exit 1; }
echo "RBJp0: Compose env-file quoting validated — RBJE_PROBE = '${RBJE_PROBE}'"

echo "RBJp1: Validate parameters"
: "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BASE_IP:?}"        && echo "RBJp0: RBRN_ENCLAVE_BASE_IP        = ${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BASE_IP}"
: "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_NETMASK:?}"        && echo "RBJp0: RBRN_ENCLAVE_NETMASK        = ${RBRN_ENCLAVE_NETMASK}"
: "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP:?}"      && echo "RBJp0: RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP      = ${RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP}"
: "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP:?}"      && echo "RBJp0: RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP      = ${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP}"
: "${RBRR_DNS_SERVER:?}"             && echo "RBJp0: RBRR_DNS_SERVER             = ${RBRR_DNS_SERVER}"
: "${RBRN_ENTRY_MODE:?}"             && echo "RBJp0: RBRN_ENTRY_MODE             = ${RBRN_ENTRY_MODE}"
: "${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION:?}" && echo "RBJp0: RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION = ${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}"
: "${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE:?}"     && echo "RBJp0: RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE     = ${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE}"
: "${RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE:?}"        && echo "RBJp0: RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE        = ${RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE}"
: "${RBRN_UPLINK_PORT_MIN:?}"        && echo "RBJp0: RBRN_UPLINK_PORT_MIN        = ${RBRN_UPLINK_PORT_MIN}"
: "${RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE:?}"     && echo "RBJp0: RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE     = ${RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE}"
: "${RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_CIDRS:?}"   && echo "RBJp0: RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_CIDRS   = ${RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_CIDRS}"
: "${RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_DOMAINS:?}" && echo "RBJp0: RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_DOMAINS = ${RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_DOMAINS}"

echo "RBJp1: Discovering network interfaces by IP (Docker does not guarantee eth0/eth1 ordering)"
z_temp_file="/tmp/rbj_iface_discovery.txt"

ip -o addr show to "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP}" > "${z_temp_file}" || exit 11
read z_num RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF z_rest < "${z_temp_file}"
test -n "${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF}" || { echo "FATAL: No interface found with IP ${RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP}"; exit 11; }

ip -o -4 addr show scope global > "${z_temp_file}" || exit 11
RBJ_UPLINK_IF=""
while read z_num z_ifname z_rest; do
  test "${z_ifname}" = "${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF}" && continue
  RBJ_UPLINK_IF="${z_ifname}"
  break
done < "${z_temp_file}"
test -n "${RBJ_UPLINK_IF}" || { echo "FATAL: No uplink interface found"; exit 11; }

rm -f "${z_temp_file}"
echo "RBJp1: Enclave interface = ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF}"
echo "RBJp1: Uplink interface  = ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF}"

# Docker assigns the container's default route to whichever attached network's
# gateway it picks (alphabetically-first attached, empirically); compose 'priority'
# is not honored. Same don't-trust-Docker discipline as interface naming above —
# sentry takes ownership: derive uplink gateway as first usable IP of uplink
# subnet (Docker bridge convention) and install default route via uplink.
echo "RBJp1: Computing uplink gateway from connected subnet"
z_uplink_addr_cidr=$(ip -o -4 addr show dev "${RBJ_UPLINK_IF}" | awk '{print $4; exit}')
test -n "${z_uplink_addr_cidr}" || { echo "FATAL: No IPv4 address on uplink interface ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF}"; exit 12; }
z_uplink_ip="${z_uplink_addr_cidr%/*}"
z_uplink_prefix="${z_uplink_addr_cidr#*/}"

IFS=. read z_o1 z_o2 z_o3 z_o4 <<EOF_OCTETS
${z_uplink_ip}
EOF_OCTETS

z_host_bits=$(( 32 - z_uplink_prefix ))
z_ip_int=$(( (z_o1 << 24) | (z_o2 << 16) | (z_o3 << 8) | z_o4 ))
z_mask=$(( 0xFFFFFFFF ^ ((1 << z_host_bits) - 1) ))
z_net_int=$(( z_ip_int & z_mask ))
z_gw_int=$(( z_net_int + 1 ))
RBJ_UPLINK_GW="$(( (z_gw_int >> 24) & 0xFF )).$(( (z_gw_int >> 16) & 0xFF )).$(( (z_gw_int >> 8) & 0xFF )).$(( z_gw_int & 0xFF ))"
echo "RBJp1: Uplink gateway     = ${RBJ_UPLINK_GW}"

echo "RBJp1: Replacing default route via uplink (sentry-side ownership)"
ip route replace default via "${RBJ_UPLINK_GW}" dev "${RBJ_UPLINK_IF}" || exit 13
ip -o -4 route show default

echo "RBJp1: Beginning IPTables initialization"

echo "RBJp1: Set ephemeral port range for uplink connections"
echo "${RBRN_UPLINK_PORT_MIN} 65535" > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_local_port_range || exit 10

echo "RBJp1: Flushing existing rules"
iptables -F        || exit 10
iptables -t nat -F || exit 10

echo "RBJp1: Setting default policies"
iptables -P INPUT   DROP || exit 10
iptables -P FORWARD DROP || exit 10
iptables -P OUTPUT  DROP || exit 10

echo "RBJp1: Configuring loopback access"
iptables -A INPUT  -i lo -j ACCEPT || exit 10
iptables -A OUTPUT -o lo -j ACCEPT || exit 10

echo "RBJp1: Setting up connection tracking"
iptables -A INPUT   -m state --state RELATED,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT || exit 10
iptables -A FORWARD -m state --state RELATED,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT || exit 10
iptables -A OUTPUT  -m state --state RELATED,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT || exit 10

echo "RBJp1: Creating RBM chains"
iptables -N RBM-INGRESS || exit 10
iptables -N RBM-EGRESS  || exit 10
iptables -N RBM-FORWARD || exit 10

echo "RBJp1: Setting up chain jumps"
iptables -A INPUT   -j RBM-INGRESS || exit 10
iptables -A OUTPUT  -j RBM-EGRESS  || exit 10
iptables -A FORWARD -j RBM-FORWARD || exit 10

echo "RBJp2: Allowing ICMP within enclave only"
iptables -A RBM-INGRESS -i ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -p icmp -j ACCEPT || exit 20
iptables -A RBM-EGRESS  -o ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -p icmp -j ACCEPT || exit 20

echo "RBJp2: Enabling IP forwarding (required whenever sentry forwards on behalf of the enclave)"
echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward || exit 25

if test "${RBRN_ENTRY_MODE}" = "rbnne_enabled"; then
  echo "RBJp2c: Relaxing rp_filter to loose mode (required for interface-agnostic ingress)"
  # rp_filter=1 (strict) requires the packet's source-IP reverse-path to match the
  # ingress interface. Inbound published-port traffic from the framework gateway
  # (e.g., Docker Desktop 192.168.65.1) arrives on whichever interface Docker chose
  # for port-publish delivery, but its reverse route is always via the default
  # gateway on uplink — strict mode mismatches and drops at routing time, before
  # the iptables FORWARD chain ever sees the packet. Loose mode (rp_filter=2)
  # allows any source IP that's reachable via any interface, which is the actual
  # invariant for entry-port traffic. Iptables rules below still gate authorization;
  # rp_filter is not the load-bearing security control.
  echo 2 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/all/rp_filter || exit 25

  echo "RBJp2c: Configuring entry-port DNAT + MASQUERADE + FORWARD-ACCEPT"
  # Classification: destination port + per-IP source exclusion of the two enclave-internal
  # containers (sentry and bottle/pentacle). RETURN short-circuit pattern — if source matches
  # either enclave container IP, skip the DNAT; otherwise the unconditional DNAT below fires.
  #
  # Why per-IP and not whole-CIDR (! -s ENCLAVE_CIDR): on linux Docker Engine the host
  # attaches to the enclave bridge as a peer with the bridge gateway IP (.1), which is inside
  # the enclave CIDR by Docker convention. Whole-CIDR exclusion rejects every legitimate host
  # SYN (empirically confirmed on cerebro: PREROUTING DNAT fired 0 times). Per-IP excludes
  # exactly the threat surface — the two enclave container IPs known to compose — and lets
  # the bridge gateway, Desktop's VM gateway (192.168.65.1), rootlesskit's synthetic source
  # (10.0.2.100), and external clients all pass.
  #
  # Invariant defended: "enclave-internal sources MUST NOT reach sentry's entry port via DNAT"
  # (ifrit's direct_sentry_probe and net_dnat_entry_reflection sorties).
  iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -p tcp --dport "${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}" \
           -s "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP}" -j RETURN || exit 25
  iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -p tcp --dport "${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}" \
           -s "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP}" -j RETURN || exit 25
  iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -p tcp --dport "${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}" \
           -j DNAT --to-destination "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP}:${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE}" || exit 25

  # Return-path symmetry: MASQUERADE rewrites the source of the post-DNAT packet to
  # sentry's enclave IP, so the bottle's reply destination is in-enclave. The bottle then
  # routes via its directly-connected enclave route back to sentry, where conntrack reverses
  # the NAT cleanly. Without this, the bottle would reply to the original (possibly
  # framework-rewritten) source IP, traversing default-via-sentry into transit — empirically
  # broken on Docker Desktop per the topology-reframe scry diagnostic.
  iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -o ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -p tcp \
           -d "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP}" --dport "${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE}" -j MASQUERADE || exit 25

  # Authorization: only flows that sentry's own PREROUTING DNAT created conntrack state
  # for are allowed to forward. Conntrack DNAT-state is unforgeable from either bridge.
  # The parent FORWARD chain's ESTABLISHED,RELATED ACCEPT (line above) handles the return
  # path before reaching RBM-FORWARD.
  iptables -A RBM-FORWARD -p tcp \
           -d "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP}" --dport "${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE}" \
           -m conntrack --ctstate DNAT -j ACCEPT || exit 25
fi

echo "RBJp2b: Blocking ICMP cross-boundary traffic"
iptables -A RBM-FORWARD         -p icmp -j DROP || exit 28
iptables -A RBM-EGRESS  -o ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} -p icmp -j DROP || exit 28

echo "RBJp3: Phase 3: Access Setup"
if test "${RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE}" = "rbnne_disabled"; then
  echo "RBJp3: Blocking all non-port traffic"
  iptables -A RBM-EGRESS  -o ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} -j DROP || exit 30
  iptables -A RBM-FORWARD -i ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -j DROP || exit 30
else
  echo "RBJp3: Setting up uplink network hardening"
  echo 0 > /proc/sys/net/ipv6/conf/all/disable_ipv6    || exit 31
  echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/${RBJ_UPLINK_IF}/route_localnet || exit 31

  echo "RBJp3: Configuring NAT"
  iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -o ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} -s "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BASE_IP}/${RBRN_ENCLAVE_NETMASK}" \
                                       ! -d "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BASE_IP}/${RBRN_ENCLAVE_NETMASK}" \
                                       -j MASQUERADE || exit 31

  if test "${RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE}" = "rbnne_global"; then
    echo "RBJp3: Enabling global access"
    iptables -A RBM-EGRESS  -o ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} -j ACCEPT || exit 31
    iptables -A RBM-FORWARD -i ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -j ACCEPT || exit 31
  else
    echo "RBJp3: Configuring DNS server access"
    iptables -A RBM-EGRESS  -o ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} -p udp --dport 53 -d "${RBRR_DNS_SERVER}"        -j ACCEPT || exit 31
    iptables -A RBM-EGRESS  -o ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} -p tcp --dport 53 -d "${RBRR_DNS_SERVER}"        -j ACCEPT || exit 31
    iptables -A RBM-FORWARD -i ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -p udp --dport 53 -d "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP}" -j ACCEPT || exit 31
    iptables -A RBM-FORWARD -i ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -p tcp --dport 53 -d "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP}" -j ACCEPT || exit 31
    iptables -A RBM-FORWARD -i ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -p udp --dport 53                                -j DROP   || exit 31
    iptables -A RBM-FORWARD -i ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -p tcp --dport 53                                -j DROP   || exit 31

    echo "RBJp3: Setting up CIDR-based access control"
    for cidr in ${RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_CIDRS}; do
      iptables -A RBM-EGRESS  -o ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} -d "${cidr}" -j ACCEPT || exit 32
      iptables -A RBM-FORWARD -i ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -d "${cidr}" -j ACCEPT || exit 32
    done
  fi
fi

echo "RBJp4: Configuring DNS services"

echo "RBJp4: Configuring sentry DNS resolution"
echo "nameserver ${RBRR_DNS_SERVER}" > /etc/resolv.conf   || exit 40

if test "${RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE}" = "rbnne_disabled"; then
  echo "RBJp4: Blocking all DNS traffic"
  iptables -A RBM-FORWARD -i ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -p udp --dport 53 -j DROP || exit 40
  iptables -A RBM-FORWARD -i ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -p tcp --dport 53 -j DROP || exit 40
  iptables -A RBM-EGRESS  -o ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} -p udp --dport 53 -j DROP || exit 40
  iptables -A RBM-EGRESS  -o ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} -p tcp --dport 53 -j DROP || exit 40
else
  echo "RBJp4: Set up DNS Server"

  echo "RBJp4: Note version in use"
  dnsmasq --version

  echo "RBJp4: Configuring dnsmasq"
  echo "bind-interfaces"                                         > /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
  echo "interface=${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF}"                             >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
  echo "listen-address=${RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP}"               >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
  echo "no-dhcp-interface=${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF}"                     >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
  echo "dns-forward-max=150"                                    >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
  echo "cache-size=1000"                                        >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
  echo "min-port=4096"                                          >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
  echo "max-port=65535"                                         >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
  echo "min-cache-ttl=600"                                      >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
  echo "max-cache-ttl=3600"                                     >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
  echo "no-resolv"                                              >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
  echo "strict-order"                                           >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
  echo "bogus-priv"                                             >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
  echo "domain-needed"                                          >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
  echo "except-interface=${RBJ_UPLINK_IF}"                       >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
  echo "log-queries=extra"                                      >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
  echo "log-facility=/var/log/dnsmasq.log"                      >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
  echo "log-dhcp"                                               >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
  echo "log-debug"                                              >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
  echo "log-async=20"                                           >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
  if test "${RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE}" = "rbnne_global"; then
    echo "RBJp4: Enabling global DNS resolution"
    echo "server=${RBRR_DNS_SERVER}"                          >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
  else
    echo "RBJp4: Resolve-then-freeze — resolve allowed domains via upstream, freeze as static entries"
    for domain in ${RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_DOMAINS}; do
      echo "RBJp4: Resolving ${domain} via ${RBRR_DNS_SERVER}"
      z_ips=$(dig +short A "${domain}" @"${RBRR_DNS_SERVER}" 2>/dev/null | grep -E '^[0-9]+\.' | sort -u)
      test -n "${z_ips}" || { echo "FATAL: Cannot resolve ${domain} via ${RBRR_DNS_SERVER}"; exit 42; }
      for z_ip in ${z_ips}; do
        echo "RBJp4: Freezing ${domain} -> ${z_ip}"
        echo "address=/${domain}/${z_ip}"                    >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
      done
    done
    # No server= lines — no forwarding path exists. Subdomain queries return frozen IPs.
    # All non-allowed domains get NXDOMAIN.
    echo "address=/#/"                                        >> /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41
  fi

  echo "RBJp4: Echo back the constructed dnsmasq config file"
  cat                                                              /etc/dnsmasq.conf || exit 41

  echo "RBJp4: Starting dnsmasq service"
  dnsmasq
  echo "RBJp4: Waiting for dnsmasq to initialize"
  sleep 2

  echo "RBJp4: Configuring DNS firewall rules"
  iptables -A RBM-INGRESS -i ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -p udp --dport 53                         -j ACCEPT || exit 43
  iptables -A RBM-INGRESS -i ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -p tcp --dport 53                         -j ACCEPT || exit 43
  iptables -A RBM-EGRESS  -o ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} -p udp --dport 53 -d "${RBRR_DNS_SERVER}" -j ACCEPT || exit 43
  iptables -A RBM-EGRESS  -o ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} -p tcp --dport 53 -d "${RBRR_DNS_SERVER}" -j ACCEPT || exit 43
fi

echo "RBJp5: Signaling health"
touch /tmp/rbjh_healthy || exit 50

echo "RBJp5: Sentry setup complete, entering hold"
exec sleep infinity

1	//axhob_operation
2	{mkr_sentry_run}
3	
4	NOTE: Sentry container creation and startup is managed by Docker Compose.
5	The compose service definition specifies the sentry image, privileged mode, dual network attachment (default + enclave with static IP), environment variables forwarded from the nameplate, and the health check.
6	
7	The sentry's `CMD` is its baked-in entrypoint script (`/opt/rbk/rbjs_sentry.sh`), COPYed into the image during the vessel build.
8	No volume mounts are used for security container scripts — the baked entrypoint is the only code that runs.
9	
10	//axhoc_completion
11	After compose starts the sentry, the baked entrypoint executes: discovers interfaces by IP, configures iptables, starts dnsmasq, and signals health.
12	The compose health check (`test -f /tmp/rbjh_healthy`) gates downstream containers.
13

1	//axhob_operation
2	{opss_sentry_start}
3	
4	NOTE: Docker Compose orchestrates the full container lifecycle declaratively.
5	The sentry starts first; its health check (`/tmp/rbjh_healthy`) gates the pentacle; the pentacle's health check (`/tmp/rbjph_healthy`) gates the bottle.
6	Network creation, container instantiation, and startup ordering are all managed by compose — the sub-operations below describe what happens inside each container at startup, not imperative orchestration steps.
7	
8	//axhos_step
9	. *Compose creates networks and starts sentry*:
10	.. Compose creates
11	{at_transit_network}
12	(`transit`) and
13	{at_enclave_network}
14	(`enclave` with static subnet from nameplate)
15	.. Sentry container starts on both networks with privileged mode
16	.. Baked-in entrypoint (`rbjs_sentry.sh`) executes
17	
18	//axhos_step
19	. *Sentry discovers interfaces and takes ownership of routing*:
20	.. Sentry uses `ip -o addr show to «enclave_ip»` to identify which interface carries the enclave IP
21	.. The other global-scope interface is identified as the uplink
22	.. All iptables rules use discovered interface names, not hardcoded eth0/eth1
23	.. NOTE: Docker Compose does not guarantee network-to-interface ordering; IP-based discovery is required
24	.. Sentry replaces the default route with one via the uplink gateway, computed as the first usable IP of the uplink subnet (Docker bridge convention)
25	.. NOTE: Docker assigns the default route to the alphabetically-first attached network's gateway (empirically, on both linux engine 28.x and mac Docker Desktop 28.x); compose `services.<svc>.networks.<net>.priority` does NOT influence this. Sentry-side ownership is the load-bearing fix — same don't-trust-Docker discipline that motivates IP-based interface discovery.
26	.. NOTE: Sentry takes ownership of any runtime-implicit behavior its security envelope depends on.
27	   This applies to interface naming (IP-based discovery, above), default-route selection (explicit override post-discovery, above), published-port target selection (iptables rules that do not depend on which interface the framework chose for delivery), and any future runtime-implicit ordering that emerges.
28	   Kernel state and sentry's own iptables are the source of truth; framework-implicit ordering is not load-bearing for the security envelope.
29	
30	//axhos_step
31	. *Sentry configures iptables*:
32	.. Default policies: DROP on INPUT, FORWARD, OUTPUT
33	.. Custom chains: RBM-INGRESS, RBM-EGRESS, RBM-FORWARD
34	.. ICMP allowed within enclave only; ICMP cross-boundary blocked
35	.. Entry-port DNAT plus POSTROUTING MASQUERADE and conntrack-DNAT-state FORWARD authorization if {rbrn_entry_mode} is enabled — see {scr_port_setup} for the iptables rules and kernel sysctls (rp_filter loose, ip_local_port_range) the entry-port path requires
36	.. Access and DNS rules per nameplate configuration (allowlist/global/disabled modes)
37	
38	//axhos_step
39	. *Sentry starts dnsmasq*:
40	.. Listens on enclave interface at
41	{rbrn_enclave_sentry_ip}
42	.. Domain filtering per
43	{rbrn_uplink_dns_mode}
44	(allowlist or global)
45	.. DNS firewall rules added to RBM-INGRESS and RBM-EGRESS
46	
47	//axhos_step
48	. *Sentry signals health*:
49	.. Creates `/tmp/rbjh_healthy`
50	.. Compose health check detects this and allows pentacle to start
51	
52	//axhoc_completion
53	After sentry is healthy, the
54	{at_sentry_container}
55	has dual network connectivity with iptables enforcement and DNS services active, ready to serve the
56	{rbsc_crucible}.
57

//axhob_operation
{opss_sentry_start}

NOTE: Docker Compose orchestrates the full container lifecycle declaratively.
The sentry starts first; its health check (`/tmp/rbjh_healthy`) gates the pentacle; the pentacle's health check (`/tmp/rbjph_healthy`) gates the bottle.
Network creation, container instantiation, and startup ordering are all managed by compose — the sub-operations below describe what happens inside each container at startup, not imperative orchestration steps.

//axhos_step
. *Compose creates networks and starts sentry*:
.. Compose creates
{at_transit_network}
(`transit`) and
{at_enclave_network}
(`enclave` with static subnet from nameplate)
.. Sentry container starts on both networks with privileged mode
.. Baked-in entrypoint (`rbjs_sentry.sh`) executes

//axhos_step
. *Sentry discovers interfaces and takes ownership of routing*:
.. Sentry uses `ip -o addr show to «enclave_ip»` to identify which interface carries the enclave IP
.. The other global-scope interface is identified as the uplink
.. All iptables rules use discovered interface names, not hardcoded eth0/eth1
.. NOTE: Docker Compose does not guarantee network-to-interface ordering; IP-based discovery is required
.. Sentry replaces the default route with one via the uplink gateway, computed as the first usable IP of the uplink subnet (Docker bridge convention)
.. NOTE: Docker assigns the default route to the alphabetically-first attached network's gateway (empirically, on both linux engine 28.x and mac Docker Desktop 28.x); compose `services.<svc>.networks.<net>.priority` does NOT influence this. Sentry-side ownership is the load-bearing fix — same don't-trust-Docker discipline that motivates IP-based interface discovery.
.. NOTE: Sentry takes ownership of any runtime-implicit behavior its security envelope depends on.
   This applies to interface naming (IP-based discovery, above), default-route selection (explicit override post-discovery, above), published-port target selection (iptables rules that do not depend on which interface the framework chose for delivery), and any future runtime-implicit ordering that emerges.
   Kernel state and sentry's own iptables are the source of truth; framework-implicit ordering is not load-bearing for the security envelope.

//axhos_step
. *Sentry configures iptables*:
.. Default policies: DROP on INPUT, FORWARD, OUTPUT
.. Custom chains: RBM-INGRESS, RBM-EGRESS, RBM-FORWARD
.. ICMP allowed within enclave only; ICMP cross-boundary blocked
.. Entry-port DNAT plus POSTROUTING MASQUERADE and conntrack-DNAT-state FORWARD authorization if {rbrn_entry_mode} is enabled — see {scr_port_setup} for the iptables rules and kernel sysctls (rp_filter loose, ip_local_port_range) the entry-port path requires
.. Access and DNS rules per nameplate configuration (allowlist/global/disabled modes)

//axhos_step
. *Sentry starts dnsmasq*:
.. Listens on enclave interface at
{rbrn_enclave_sentry_ip}
.. Domain filtering per
{rbrn_uplink_dns_mode}
(allowlist or global)
.. DNS firewall rules added to RBM-INGRESS and RBM-EGRESS

//axhos_step
. *Sentry signals health*:
.. Creates `/tmp/rbjh_healthy`
.. Compose health check detects this and allows pentacle to start

//axhoc_completion
After sentry is healthy, the
{at_sentry_container}
has dual network connectivity with iptables enforcement and DNS services active, ready to serve the
{rbsc_crucible}.

1	//axhob_operation
2	{scr_port_setup}
3	
4	This operation sets kernel network state for sentry's forwarding role.
5	IP forwarding (Step 1) and the ephemeral port range (Step 2) are unconditional.
6	Reverse path filtering (Step 3) and the entry-port iptables rules (Steps 4-6) apply when {rbrn_entry_mode} is enabled; if {rbrn_entry_mode} is disabled, those steps are skipped.
7	No userspace proxy is involved; the kernel forwards packets directly.
8	
9	//axhos_step
10	. IP Forwarding:
11	* Enable IPv4 forwarding via `/proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward`
12	
13	//axhos_step
14	. Ephemeral Port Range:
15	* Set the kernel's ephemeral port auto-allocation floor to {rbrn_uplink_port_min} via `/proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_local_port_range` (floor through 65535)
16	* Keeps low ports clear of NAT and access-control rules; {rbrn_entry_port_workstation} and {rbrn_entry_port_enclave} must both be below {rbrn_uplink_port_min}
17	
18	//axhos_step
19	. Reverse Path Filtering:
20	* Set rp_filter to loose mode (value 2) on all interfaces via `/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/all/rp_filter`
21	* Strict mode (value 1) requires the source-IP reverse-path to match the ingress interface.
22	  Inbound published-port traffic from the framework's gateway arrives on whichever interface the framework chose for port-publish delivery, but its reverse route is always via the default gateway on the interface attached to {at_transit_network} — strict mode mismatches and drops at routing time, before the FORWARD chain ever sees the packet.
23	  Loose mode allows any source IP that is reachable via any interface, which is the actual invariant for entry-port traffic.
24	  Iptables rules below still gate authorization; rp_filter is not the load-bearing security control.
25	
26	//axhos_step
27	. DNAT Rules:
28	* Add three PREROUTING rules in nat table, each matching protocol TCP and destination port {rbrn_entry_port_workstation}:
29	** Source {rbrn_enclave_sentry_ip}: RETURN (short-circuits the DNAT)
30	** Source {rbrn_enclave_bottle_ip}: RETURN (short-circuits the DNAT)
31	** No source filter: DNAT to {rbrn_enclave_bottle_ip}:{rbrn_entry_port_enclave}
32	* The per-IP source exclusion of the two enclave-internal containers is the load-bearing classification axis.
33	  Whole-CIDR exclusion of the enclave subnet is incorrect: on linux Docker Engine the host attaches to the enclave bridge as a peer with the bridge gateway IP, which is inside the enclave CIDR; whole-CIDR exclusion rejects every legitimate host SYN.
34	  Per-IP exclusion narrows the rejection to exactly the threat surface and lets the bridge gateway, the framework VM gateway, the rootlesskit synthetic source, and external clients all pass.
35	* Invariant defended: enclave-internal sources MUST NOT reach the entry port via DNAT.
36	
37	//axhos_step
38	. Forward Filter:
39	* Add rule to RBM-FORWARD accepting DNATted entry traffic:
40	** Protocol TCP
41	** Destination {rbrn_enclave_bottle_ip}
42	** Destination port {rbrn_entry_port_enclave}
43	** Conntrack state DNAT (`-m conntrack --ctstate DNAT`)
44	* The conntrack DNAT-state match IS the authorization: only flows that sentry's own PREROUTING DNAT created conntrack state for are allowed to forward.
45	  Conntrack DNAT-state is unforgeable from either bridge.
46	* The return path is handled by the parent FORWARD chain's RELATED,ESTABLISHED ACCEPT (see {scr_iptables_init} state tracking) before reaching RBM-FORWARD.
47	
48	//axhos_step
49	. MASQUERADE Rule:
50	* Add POSTROUTING rule in nat table:
51	** Output interface attached to {at_enclave_network}
52	** Protocol TCP
53	** Destination {rbrn_enclave_bottle_ip}
54	** Destination port {rbrn_entry_port_enclave}
55	** MASQUERADE (rewrites source to sentry enclave IP)
56	* Return-path symmetry: the MASQUERADE rewrites the source of the post-DNAT packet to sentry's enclave IP, so the bottle's reply destination is in-enclave.
57	  The bottle then routes via its directly-connected enclave route back to sentry, where conntrack reverses the NAT cleanly.
58	  Without this, the bottle would reply to the original (possibly framework-rewritten) source IP, traversing default-via-sentry into transit — empirically broken on the Docker Desktop runtime.
59

file.content:
//axhob_operation
{scr_port_setup}

This operation sets kernel network state for sentry's forwarding role.
IP forwarding (Step 1) and the ephemeral port range (Step 2) are unconditional.
Reverse path filtering (Step 3) and the entry-port iptables rules (Steps 4-6) apply when {rbrn_entry_mode} is enabled; if {rbrn_entry_mode} is disabled, those steps are skipped.
No userspace proxy is involved; the kernel forwards packets directly.

//axhos_step
. IP Forwarding:
* Enable IPv4 forwarding via `/proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward`

//axhos_step
. Ephemeral Port Range:
* Set the kernel's ephemeral port auto-allocation floor to {rbrn_uplink_port_min} via `/proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_local_port_range` (floor through 65535)
* Keeps low ports clear of NAT and access-control rules; {rbrn_entry_port_workstation} and {rbrn_entry_port_enclave} must both be below {rbrn_uplink_port_min}

//axhos_step
. Reverse Path Filtering:
* Set rp_filter to loose mode (value 2) on all interfaces via `/proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/all/rp_filter`
* Strict mode (value 1) requires the source-IP reverse-path to match the ingress interface.
  Inbound published-port traffic from the framework's gateway arrives on whichever interface the framework chose for port-publish delivery, but its reverse route is always via the default gateway on the interface attached to {at_transit_network} — strict mode mismatches and drops at routing time, before the FORWARD chain ever sees the packet.
  Loose mode allows any source IP that is reachable via any interface, which is the actual invariant for entry-port traffic.
  Iptables rules below still gate authorization; rp_filter is not the load-bearing security control.

//axhos_step
. DNAT Rules:
* Add three PREROUTING rules in nat table, each matching protocol TCP and destination port {rbrn_entry_port_workstation}:
** Source {rbrn_enclave_sentry_ip}: RETURN (short-circuits the DNAT)
** Source {rbrn_enclave_bottle_ip}: RETURN (short-circuits the DNAT)
** No source filter: DNAT to {rbrn_enclave_bottle_ip}:{rbrn_entry_port_enclave}
* The per-IP source exclusion of the two enclave-internal containers is the load-bearing classification axis.
  Whole-CIDR exclusion of the enclave subnet is incorrect: on linux Docker Engine the host attaches to the enclave bridge as a peer with the bridge gateway IP, which is inside the enclave CIDR; whole-CIDR exclusion rejects every legitimate host SYN.
  Per-IP exclusion narrows the rejection to exactly the threat surface and lets the bridge gateway, the framework VM gateway, the rootlesskit synthetic source, and external clients all pass.
* Invariant defended: enclave-internal sources MUST NOT reach the entry port via DNAT.

//axhos_step
. Forward Filter:
* Add rule to RBM-FORWARD accepting DNATted entry traffic:
** Protocol TCP
** Destination {rbrn_enclave_bottle_ip}
** Destination port {rbrn_entry_port_enclave}
** Conntrack state DNAT (`-m conntrack --ctstate DNAT`)
* The conntrack DNAT-state match IS the authorization: only flows that sentry's own PREROUTING DNAT created conntrack state for are allowed to forward.
  Conntrack DNAT-state is unforgeable from either bridge.
* The return path is handled by the parent FORWARD chain's RELATED,ESTABLISHED ACCEPT (see {scr_iptables_init} state tracking) before reaching RBM-FORWARD.

//axhos_step
. MASQUERADE Rule:
* Add POSTROUTING rule in nat table:
** Output interface attached to {at_enclave_network}
** Protocol TCP
** Destination {rbrn_enclave_bottle_ip}
** Destination port {rbrn_entry_port_enclave}
** MASQUERADE (rewrites source to sentry enclave IP)
* Return-path symmetry: the MASQUERADE rewrites the source of the post-DNAT packet to sentry's enclave IP, so the bottle's reply destination is in-enclave.
  The bottle then routes via its directly-connected enclave route back to sentry, where conntrack reverses the NAT cleanly.
  Without this, the bottle would reply to the original (possibly framework-rewritten) source IP, traversing default-via-sentry into transit — empirically broken on the Docker Desktop runtime.

content.text:
The read is done — source and all five touching specs. Here's the pilot's headline, then the findings.

## Headline

Every dense comment in RBJS already has a spec home carrying nearly the same wording. This pair's problem is not unhomed knowledge (ACGm_104's assumed case) — it's six **cross-medium paraphrase forks**: full design rationale written out twice, once in the script, once in RBSPT/RBSSS/RBSDS, drifting independently. The specs are consistently equal-or-better (more general, version-scoped). That reframes the Palisade question nicely — details below.

## Findings (detect-only, nothing touched)

| # | Site (rbjs_sentry.sh) | Content | Move | Receiving home |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | :53-57 | Docker default-route comment ("alphabetically-first, priority not honored, sentry takes ownership") | ACGm_104 → citation-collapse | RBSSS step 2 NOTEs — already carries it, *more* precisely (engine/Desktop 28.x scoping) |
| F2 | :122-130 | rp_filter loose-mode rationale | ACGm_104 → citation-collapse | RBSPT step 3 — near-verbatim, spec generalized ("framework's gateway" vs comment's hardcoded 192.168.65.1) |
| F3 | :133-147 | DNAT per-IP-vs-whole-CIDR classification | ACGm_104 → citation-collapse | RBSPT step 4 — carries the rationale and the invariant sentence; comment-only extras are the cerebro detail, concrete IPs, and the two ifrit sortie names |
| F4 | :157-160 | MASQUERADE return-path symmetry | ACGm_104 → citation-collapse | RBSPT step 6 — near-verbatim |
| F5 | :164-167 | conntrack-DNAT-state-as-authorization | ACGm_104 → citation-collapse | RBSPT step 5 — near-verbatim including "unforgeable from either bridge" |
| F6 | :265-266 | no-server=-lines / NXDOMAIN comment | ACGm_104 → citation-collapse | RBSDS resolve-then-freeze bullet — spec fuller (names the exfil channel) |

**In passing for the corpus sweeps:**
- *ACGm_105 seeds*: the invariant sentence ("enclave-internal sources MUST NOT reach the entry port via DNAT") worded in both RBJS:146 and RBSPT:35; the "don't-trust-Docker discipline" phrase in RBJS:55 and RBSSS:25. Also one intra-spec near-dup: RBSSS:25's tail restates the ownership doctrine that the very next NOTE (RBSSS:26-28) states in general form.
- *ACGm_107-adjacent*: RBJS:160 "per the topology-reframe scry diagnostic" and RBJS:141 "empirically confirmed on cerebro: PREROUTING DNAT fired 0 times" — history-time provenance stranded in durable source; home is the commit/memo.
- *ACGm_106*: the spec side is clean — RBSPT/RBSII/RBSDS read as constraint throughout; no unmarked ornament found worth draining.
- *Non-ACG, noting only*: RBJS:19-31 echo lines say `RBJp0:` under the `RBJp1: Validate parameters` header — a phase-prefix inconsistency in announcements, out of this pace's scope.

## The Palisade ruling (the question this pilot exists to settle)

The three-homes table licenses edit-time comments to carry "Palisade characterization a maintainer needs *at the code*." These comments are genuinely that — Docker's route selection, the host-as-bridge-peer quirk, Desktop's gateway IP. But the specs *also* carry the full characterization. So the live question isn't "is this Palisade content?" (it is) — it's **whether the Palisade license makes the edit-time comment a co-equal home or a fallback home.**

My recommendation: **fallback, not co-equal.** Palisade characterization obeys the same single-authoritative-wording rule as everything else; when a spec home exists, the spec is authoritative and the code-side residue is the *tripwire* — one or two lines stating what not to "fix" plus the citation. Concretely for F3, something like:

```
# Per-IP RETURN, deliberately NOT whole-CIDR — whole-CIDR rejects every host
# SYN on linux Engine (host is a bridge peer inside the enclave CIDR).
# Full classification rationale: RBSPT "DNAT Rules".
```

What the maintainer needs at the code is the don't-undo-the-weird-rule warning; the why-lecture has a forcing function only at the spec. The honest counter-consideration: this script ships baked into the vessel, so the runtime copy is read spec-less — but maintenance happens repo-side, and the in-container reader is debugging execution, which the rich echo announcements already serve.

## Proposed ACG amendments (three)

1. **ACGm_104 — name the already-homed case.** The move text assumes the home must be created/extended. Add: when the receiving home already carries the content, the eviction degenerates to *citation-collapse* — declare the authoritative side (move discipline rule 1), truncate the comment to residue + citation. This pair shows it's the common case, not the edge.
2. **Three-homes table — bound the Palisade license.** Add the ruling above: edit-time is the fallback home for Palisade characterization, authoritative only when no spec home exists; otherwise the comment keeps signature + tripwire + citation.
3. **Silent spot — test-name references in comments.** F3's comment names two ifrit sorties as the invariant's defenders. ACG says nothing about verifier pointers in source comments; sortie names drift with renames and nothing forces the comment to follow. My lean: the linkage belongs in the test (sortie cites the spec quoin it defends), never in source comments — but this one I'd flag as genuinely open rather than recommend firmly.

How do you want to rule on the Palisade boundary — fallback-home as I've recommended, or do you see the baked-into-the-vessel angle (or something else) as grounds for keeping fuller characterization at the code?

message.content: I have gotten tired and need you to present your findings to me in smaller chunks with simpler language, one by one.  You'll need to remind me of appropriate context from ACG and the paddock and the docket for each chunk... try this

content.text:
Sure. Let's go one piece at a time — I'll keep each piece small, and you say "next" when ready.

**Chunk 1 of 6 — the big picture finding.**

Context reminder: this pace audits the sentry script (the one that sets up iptables and DNS inside the sentry container) against its specs. ACG's core rule is "a fact should live in exactly one home, and everything else should point at that home."

What I found: the sentry script has six big explanatory comments — the rp_filter one, the DNAT one, and so on. I expected to find comments whose knowledge had *no* home in the specs, which would mean writing new spec text. Instead, **every single one of those comments already has a spec home saying nearly the same thing** — RBSPT (port setup) especially. Someone (probably past-us) wrote the rationale twice: once in the script, once in the spec.

Why that matters: two copies of the same explanation drift apart over time, and a future reader can't tell which one is law. The fix direction is simple: pick the spec as the official copy, shrink each comment down to a short pointer.

The good news: the spec copies are consistently *better* than the comment copies — more general, more carefully scoped. So nothing needs to be written; things mostly need to be shrunk.

That's the whole headline. Say "next" and I'll walk you through the first specific comment.

message.content: The vision and intent of the split between spec and code is that the spec should be basically implementation language independent yet very crisp and with detailed constraints where needed in order to reason about the steps without having to decode code, whereas the code shouldn't have to carry all the reasoning artifacts.  I have a very strong interest in dividing concerns so that I can wrangle larger and larger problem sets in LLM limited context.  Given this specific recommendation, are you inclined to reduce duplication?  Do not make changes now by the way, we are reasoning out this initiative with precise case examples.  Thoughts?

content.text:
Yes — and your framing actually sharpens *why*, beyond the drift argument I led with.

If the goal is wrangling bigger problems in limited context, then the question for every fact becomes: **which reading occasion pays for it?** Code gets loaded constantly — every edit, every debug, every agent doing a quick fix. The spec gets loaded when someone is reasoning about design. Duplicating rationale into the code means the most-frequently-loaded medium carries the heaviest cargo, and an agent that loads both must spend context *reconciling* two wordings on top of reading them. The duplication taxes exactly the resource you're trying to conserve.

So: strongly inclined to reduce. The spec side of this pair already does what you describe — RBSPT states the DNAT classification crisply, implementation-language-free, with the constraints needed to reason about it without reading a single iptables flag.

The one thing I'd keep at the code, and I want to be precise about what it is: not reasoning, but a **tripwire**. The per-IP RETURN rules *look like* a clumsy way to write a whole-CIDR exclusion. An editor who arrives without the spec loaded — which is the normal case — will be tempted to "simplify" them. A two-line comment saying "deliberately not whole-CIDR; load RBSPT 'DNAT Rules' before touching" isn't a duplicate of the reasoning — it's the signal that forces the spec load at exactly the moment it's needed. It's cheap in tokens and it's the only thing standing between a confident agent and a regression.

That gives a clean division rule for your context-budget goal: **the code carries what an editor needs before deciding to load the spec; the spec carries everything needed after.** Signature + warning + pointer at the code; all reasoning at the spec; nothing twice.

That's my position. Want to test it against the first concrete comment (the Docker default-route one), or keep pushing on the principle?

content: ACG audit pilot on the sentry pair (₢BbAAI): all six big script comments duplicate spec text, and we agreed code keeps only short tripwire pointers. Next: walk the first concrete comment, the Docker default-route one.

message.content: Very interesting observation... makes me think a good direction for ACG to go is to identify a requirement naming discipline that places anchors in specs which code can reference in such a 'tripwire' place.  Thoughts?

content.text:
I think it's the right direction, and the ecosystem already has most of the parts — what's new is pointing them at this job.

The strongest argument for it: **grep becomes a bidirectional census.** Today the tripwire would say "see RBSPT 'DNAT Rules'" — a prose pointer to a section title that can be reworded silently, and the spec has no idea who depends on it. If instead the invariant carries a minted anchor — the spec defines it once, the code comment cites the bare name — then one grep returns the definition site *and* every code site that defends it. From code, an agent loads exactly the right spec fragment instead of the whole document; from spec, you can see what breaks if a constraint changes. That's directly your context-wrangling goal: precise, small, on-demand loads instead of whole-document hauls.

It also resolves the question I'd left open in finding F3. The comment there names two ifrit sorties as the invariant's defenders — fragile, since sortie names drift. Under your scheme, the spec anchors the invariant, the comment cites the anchor, and *the sorties cite it too*. One grep then returns: definition, implementation sites, and tests. Three-way census, no registry to maintain.

And it fits ACG's existing frame rather than fighting it: the move discipline already says "concept linkage is name-identity," with source-to-spec comments as the licensed exception — this disciplines the exception. MCM already owns the anchor mechanics. The guide family already does cited-rule IDs (PCG's `PCr_`, CBG's rule families, ACG's own `ACGm_`) on the principle that *an ID earns its existence only when a citer will exist*.

That principle is also the main caution: mint the anchor **when the tripwire is written**, demand-driven, never a speculative sweep anchoring every spec step. Otherwise you get requirement-ID bureaucracy — hundreds of anchors nobody cites. The second design question to settle later, not tonight: what the anchor *is* mechanically — a new quoin category for invariants, or anchors hung on existing step structure. That's an MCM/AXLA interface decision and deserves its own thinking.

So my proposal: this goes into the findings as a fourth ACG amendment — direction agreed, mechanism deferred to its own pace. Sound right?

message.content: MCM identifies quoin and sprue; did we just mull creating a new thing at that level?  Each of those has functional domains, but maybe we just identified a whole new category.  Thoughts?  We probably ought to finish this sequential presentation process before we commit to changes though.

content.text:
I think yes — and the tell is that the *referent type* is different, not just the domain.

A **quoin** names a concept — a noun, a thing in the model. A **sprue** carries a name through code-space — it's a census channel for one format or family. What we just described homes a **proposition**: a constraint that must hold. Its citers aren't readers using a term; they're *defenders declaring allegiance* — the code site that implements the invariant, the sortie that attacks it, the comment that warns you off breaking it. Quoins answer "what is this?"; this thing answers "what must stay true, and who is bound by it?"

You *could* fake it with an ordinary quoin — mint one whose definition text is the invariant. But quoin machinery is built for vocabulary: display text, variants, prose substitution. An invariant doesn't want to be woven into sentences; it wants a stable ID that three different media can grep for. Bending quoins to carry it would blur the census that makes each mechanism work — the same argument the fourth clause makes about two formats sharing a sprue.

So: probably a genuine third category at the MCM level, with its own annotation/voicing story and eventually its own minted word — none of which we should design tired, tonight. Agreed on not committing.

Let me park it precisely so it survives: I'm holding **four proposed amendments** now — the ACGm_104 already-homed case, the Palisade fallback-home ruling, the verifier-pointer question (now likely *answered by* this mechanism), and this one: a proposed new MCM-level category for cited invariants, mechanism deferred. All detect-only, nothing touched.

Back to the walk-through, then. **Chunk 2 of 6** is the Docker default-route comment — the gentlest of the six and a clean warm-up for the pattern. Ready?

lastPrompt: MCM identifies quoin and sprue; did we just mull creating a new thing at that level?  Each of those has functional domains, but maybe we just identified a whole new category.  Thoughts?  We probably o…

message.content: ready, please continue

content.text:
**Chunk 2 of 6 — the Docker default-route comment (F1).**

Context reminder: when the sentry container starts, it sits on two networks — the enclave (where the bottle lives) and the uplink (toward the outside world). Docker decides on its own which network gets the container's default route, and Docker's choice can't be trusted. So the script overrides it: it computes the uplink gateway itself and installs its own route.

The script carries a five-line comment (lines 53–57) explaining all this: Docker picks the alphabetically-first network, the compose `priority` setting doesn't work, so sentry takes ownership.

The finding: **the spec already says all of this, and says it better.** RBSSS (the sentry-start spec) has two NOTEs covering the same ground — and the spec version is actually more precise: it records *which Docker versions* this was observed on (engine 28.x, Desktop 28.x), which the comment doesn't.

So this is the pattern I flagged in chunk 1: same reasoning written twice, and the copy in the spec is the better one. Under the rule we just agreed — code carries only what an editor needs *before* loading the spec — the comment here would shrink to roughly two lines: "Docker's route choice isn't trusted — we install our own; see RBSSS step 2 before changing." (Or, in the future, a citation of a minted invariant anchor.)

One extra wrinkle worth one sentence: the comment's phrase "same don't-trust-Docker discipline" *also* appears in the spec — so this site is a seed for the paraphrase-collapse corpus sweep too (ACGm_105), which a later pace handles; we just note it.

That's the whole of F1. The next one, rp_filter, is the first of the genuinely security-dense ones. Say "next" when ready.

content: We're auditing the sentry script against its specs for ACG (₢BbAAI), walking six duplicate-comment findings one at a time; chunk 2 of 6 (Docker default-route) is done. Next: say "next" for chunk 3, the rp_filter comment.

message.content:
Two stray thoughts for your consideration:

What if our naming convention for these spec/code crosslinks is RBs_ prefixed?  Purely distinct from existing quoins and sprues.  I don't have inspiration whether to make the postfix be more like silks or more like a packed unique dense thing like firemarks or coronets.  Thoughts?  A key thing for our discussion today is that I will _not_ be releasing the specs into the open source: too much of revealing my tricks.  A good boundary here will help, that sort of obscures what is going on (or makes it easy to release just key sections, on need).

Another goal of thsi partittioning is to work out processes to improve the various RCG/BCG/ACG/CBG documents by running trials of 'regenerate from specs' with variously updated guides such that cheaper and faster models can be used.  This will accelerate our work together.

Thoughts?  Go ahead to the next chunk too

content.text:
Both stray thoughts land well — quick takes on each, then chunk 3.

**On `RBs_` and the suffix shape.** Distinct-from-quoin-and-sprue is right, and the mixed-case form is genuinely greppable as a category marker. On silks-vs-dense: your closed-spec boundary actually *decides* this one, I think. The code side is released; the anchor name travels with it. A readable suffix like `RBs_dnat_per_ip_exclusion` leaks the trick in the name itself — an outsider greps your open code and reads your security reasoning off the identifiers. A packed form (`RBs_Bk7`-ish) makes the open-source side carry an inert token: the tripwire sentence stays human-readable ("deliberate — do not change without consulting"), but the *why* is unresolvable without the closed spec. That's exactly your "obscure what's going on, release key sections on need" boundary — and releasing one section never leaks the semantics of the rest of the namespace. Costs to note honestly: dense IDs need an allocator to avoid collisions (vvx minting them, the way jjx mints coronets, is the obvious shape), and they're unmemorable in-context — a price you're already comfortable paying with firemarks. Two flags for later: house convention says anchors are lowercase snake_case, so a mixed-case anchor is a deliberate convention break to record; and `rbs*` lowercase is partially occupied (`rbsc_crucible` etc.), so the mint needs the usual grep gate.

**On regenerate-from-specs trials.** This is the strongest argument yet for everything we've settled today, because it gives specs the thing ACG says edit-time comments fatally lack: a *forcing function*. If a cheaper model must regenerate conformant code from spec + guide alone, then every fact living only in a code comment is invisible to the trial — the trial mechanically punishes exactly the misallocation this audit hunts. And regeneration diffs localize beautifully to invariant anchors: "the regenerated code broke `RBs_X`" is a measurable guide-quality signal. I'd note one sequencing implication: the dedup work we're reasoning through isn't just hygiene anymore — it's the *prerequisite* for that acceleration strategy. Worth a line in the paddock when we land things.

---

**Chunk 3 of 6 — the rp_filter comment (F2).**

Context reminder: rp_filter is a kernel anti-spoofing knob. In strict mode, the kernel drops any packet whose source address wouldn't route back out the interface it came in on. The sentry script *loosens* it (mode 2) when the entry port is enabled.

Why: published-port traffic from the host arrives on whichever interface Docker happens to deliver it to, but the reply route always goes via the uplink. Strict mode sees that mismatch and silently drops the packet — *before* iptables ever sees it. The script's nine-line comment (lines 122–130) explains this and ends with the key sentence: rp_filter is *not* the load-bearing security control — the iptables rules below still do the real authorization.

The finding: same pattern as F1. RBSPT step 3 carries the identical reasoning, generalized (the spec says "the framework's gateway" where the comment hardcodes Docker Desktop's 192.168.65.1). Citation-collapse: spec is authoritative, comment shrinks to a tripwire.

And this site shows *why* the tripwire matters more than anywhere else so far: the trap is a security-minded editor seeing `rp_filter=2` and "hardening" it back to strict. That breaks ingress in the nastiest possible way — drops happen at routing time, invisible to iptables logging, brutal to diagnose. The two lines that survive at the code are precisely: "loose is deliberate; strict silently kills entry-port traffic before the firewall sees it — consult ‹anchor› before changing."

Say "next" for F3, the DNAT classification — the densest of the six and the one that raised the test-name question.

message.content: Thank you for these smaller chunks, this process is working well.  Next please

content.text:
Glad it's working. **Chunk 4 of 6 — the DNAT classification comment (F3), the densest of the six.**

Context reminder: when the entry port is enabled, outside clients connect to the sentry's published port, and iptables DNAT forwards them to the bottle. But there's an invariant to defend: *the enclave's own containers must never reach that entry port from the inside* — that would be a way for the bottle to bounce traffic off its own front door. The ifrit attack suite (our in-container pentester) has two sorties that specifically attack this.

How the script defends it: two narrow rules that exempt exactly the sentry IP and the bottle IP from the DNAT, then a catch-all DNAT for everyone else. The fifteen-line comment (lines 133–147) explains the design choice that makes this look odd: you'd *think* the clean way is one rule excluding the whole enclave subnet. But on Linux Docker, the host itself sits on the enclave bridge with an address inside that subnet — so a whole-subnet exclusion rejects every legitimate connection from the host. Per-IP exclusion is the load-bearing choice, not a clumsy version of the subnet one.

The finding: RBSPT step 4 already carries the rationale *and* the invariant sentence, generalized. Citation-collapse again. But this comment has three extras the spec deliberately doesn't carry, and they're worth a sentence each:

- **"Empirically confirmed on cerebro: PREROUTING DNAT fired 0 times"** — that's the debugging story, history-time knowledge. Its home is the commit/memo, not the durable script. It just gets dropped (it survives in git).
- **The concrete IPs** (Desktop's 192.168.65.1, rootlesskit's 10.0.2.100) — useful color, but the spec's general wording ("the framework VM gateway, the rootlesskit synthetic source") is the durable form. Drop from the comment.
- **The two ifrit sortie names** — this was my open question from the first pass: do test names belong in a source comment? They drift when tests rename, and nothing forces the comment to follow. Your anchor idea answers it: the invariant gets an `RBs_` anchor; the comment cites the anchor, the *sorties* cite the anchor, the spec defines it. One grep then returns definition, defending rules, and attacking tests — and no name in the comment can go stale.

This is also the site where the tripwire earns its keep most: per-IP rules look exactly like something an agent would "simplify" into the whole-subnet form, which is the one empirically-proven-broken variant.

Say "next" for chunk 5 — the two return-path comments (MASQUERADE and conntrack), which I'll take together since they're the same story.

message.content:
More stray thoughts as I process your findings: locate the diptych memo and read it because this new tripwire anchor coheres very well with a 'linting' process that I intend to develop soon.  That could be the tool that helps allocate these RBs_ things correctly and consistently.

I'm musing we are starting to venture into different categories of these things, which we could preserve in the text pseudo URI with another prefix letter: RBsX_ where 'X' is the category.  Do you have thoughts on identifying these categories?  Technically they could be left out of the pseudo URI and just kept in the spec.

Very interesting to note a memo origin... and I muse that as soon as we are tempted to do that, that is a sign we need formal specification instead of memo homing.  I generally use memos as temporary vessels, largely unconstrained for easy production, but not with long term foundations.

Thoughts?  and next please
